claim: AI is enabling the discovery of zero-day exploits at scale; the first known AI-discovered zero-day exploit was detected in the wild by Google Threat Intelligence
actionable: Assume that AI-discovered zero-days are now in active use by threat actors; prioritize using AI to harden your own systems (GPT-5.5 Cyber, Mythos) before attackers use discovered exploits

claim: Shy Halud NPM worm uses a dead man's switch that nukes your home directory when you revoke a stolen GitHub token; it spreads across NPM and PyPI with 373+ malicious package versions
actionable: Review all dependencies in your Node/Python projects; rotate GitHub tokens immediately; use supply chain scanning tools; avoid vibe coding without dependency review

claim: AI is accelerating cyber attacks through two mechanisms: enabling vibe coders to write more unreviewed code with more vulnerabilities, and enabling attackers to create exploit tools at scale
actionable: If using AI coding tools, implement security reviews of generated code; audit your dependencies; implement supply chain scanning and runtime monitoring

claim: Vercel was breached through an AI platform (contacts.ai) compromise; attackers showed surprising velocity and in-depth understanding, suggesting significant AI acceleration
actionable: Audit third-party AI platform integrations; use isolated credentials with minimal permissions; assume AI-accelerated attacks will be faster and more sophisticated than traditional attacks

claim: AI doesn't create vulnerabilities; it accelerates their discovery. These vulnerabilities exist because humans can't think perfectly about software; AI just finds them faster
actionable: Understand that AI-assisted vulnerability discovery is inevitable; defensive AI models will find vulnerabilities faster than attackers, creating a temporary defense advantage

claim: Supply chain attacks through NPM dependencies are becoming a primary initial access vector; Team PCP stole credentials that teams haven't rotated, enabling continued attacks
actionable: Implement dependency scanning (npm audit, Snyk, Socket); rotate credentials from any recent breaches; use principle of least privilege for CI/CD tokens

claim: The ROI of attacking smaller targets becomes profitable with AI; while total attack value is lower, the cost and effort to execute attacks drops dramatically, making long-tail attacks viable
actionable: Assume you are a profitable target even as a small company or individual; implement basic security hygiene (2FA, secret rotation, dependency scanning, network segmentation)

claim: State actors with access to frontier AI models will have better defensive AI than rogue groups with weaker models; better models are better at defense than weaker models are at attack
actionable: Understand that countries with resources for frontier AI (US, China) will have better defenses; smaller groups will attack lower-value targets where ROI is positive

claim: Anthropic's decision not to release Mythos (10 trillion parameter cyber defense model) was fear-based marketing; OpenAI released GPT-5.5 Cyber with similar capabilities and the world didn't end
actionable: Evaluate cyber defense models on technical merits rather than artificial scarcity; both o1 and GPT-5.5 Cyber are effective at finding vulnerabilities and defending systems

claim: Frontline labs are proactively developing cyber defense models (Mythos, GPT-5.5 Cyber, Daybreak); these models can find and patch vulnerabilities before attackers exploit them
actionable: Use frontier cyber models (GPT-5.5 Cyber, OpenAI Daybreak) to harden critical systems; vulnerability patching happens before mass exploitation in many cases

claim: Future state: all software vulnerabilities will be discovered by AI, then patched by AI, leaving new AI-written software with few or no vulnerabilities within 2-3 years
actionable: Invest in AI-driven security scanning and automated patching; understand that software security will shift from manual code review to AI-hardened systems
